1: The experiment with pleasure — I will test you with pleasure; enjoy yourself — is a systematic empirical approach. The Egyptian Harper's Song makes the same proposal: celebrate the festive day; let no heart be sad. Qohelet shares the proposal but evaluates it rather than simply endorsing it. The Gilgamesh epic similarly has Siduri advise Gilgamesh to find pleasure as consolation for mortality; Qohelet tests this advice and finds it ultimately inadequate on its own terms.
2: I said of laughter, it is madness, and of pleasure, what does this achieve? — the evaluation of sensory pleasure as inadequate. The Mesopotamian wisdom tradition noted the same: the Gilgamesh epic shows the pursuit of pleasure before the death of Enkidu forces Gilgamesh to confront mortality. The inadequacy of pleasure as a life-organizing principle was a stock wisdom observation; Qohelet's systematic testing makes it empirical rather than inherited tradition.
3: The experiment with wine — my heart guiding me with wisdom — is a controlled investigation: Qohelet is not simply drinking but observing the effect of wine on the pursuit of the good. Wine in the ANE was both a symbol of blessing (Psalms 104:15: wine that gladdens the heart of man) and a social lubricant for royal banquets. The wisdom tradition always paired enjoyment with discernment; Qohelet's experiment maintains the observer's distance even while testing the pleasure.
4: The building projects — houses, vineyards — directly echo Solomon's construction program in 1 Kings 7 (his own palace, the Temple, the house of the forest of Lebanon). The catalogue of achievements mirrors the conventions of royal building inscriptions throughout the ancient Near East, where kings listed their works as evidence of divine favor. Qohelet inhabits this tradition ironically: the list meant to demonstrate lasting achievement is presented as evidence for hevel.
5: Gardens and parks (pardesim — the Persian loanword for paradise) were symbols of royal achievement: the hanging gardens of Babylon, the royal gardens of Assyrian kings, Egyptian pleasure gardens. Planting all kinds of fruit trees was a display of the king's command over nature. The Persian term pardes situates the text in or near the Persian period, and the royal garden imagery connects to Eden (Genesis 2:8-9: a garden in Eden with every tree good for food).
6: Irrigation pools and forests reflect water management achievements in the Jerusalem hills. The Pools of Siloam and ancient cisterns remain in the archaeological record. More broadly, the management of water — diverting it to irrigate planted forests — represented the ancient king's power to order the natural world. Water management was a mark of civilization and royal power throughout the ANE; Qohelet's Solomonic persona claims this achievement as part of his comprehensive experiment.
7: The inventory of servants, flocks, silver, gold, and entertainers precisely mirrors the description of Solomon's wealth in 1 Kings 10:14-29 and the conventions of royal inventory lists. Sennacherib's annals and the Amarna letters both catalog royal possessions in similar terms. Male and female singers were entertainment-servants common in ancient courts; Mesopotamian king lists reference royal singers and musicians as markers of civilization and prosperity.
8: The gathering of silver and gold from the treasure of kings and provinces alludes to the tribute system sustaining Solomon's court (1 Kings 10:15: besides what came from traders and merchants, and from all the kings of Arabia and the governors of the land). The accumulation of wealth as the measure of royal success was universal in the ANE; Qohelet tests whether full realization of this ideal satisfies the yitron question. His conclusion: it does not.
9: My wisdom also remained with me — a methodologically important parenthetical: the experiment was conducted with controlled observation, not abandonment. The ancient wisdom tradition valued the combination of experience and reflective distance; the sage who can taste pleasures without being consumed by them is the ideal wisdom figure. Qohelet presents himself as this controlled observer, which gives his negative evaluation its credibility.
10: Whatever my eyes desired I did not withhold — the fullest possible testing of desire satisfaction. The Gilgamesh epic's heroic fulfillment arc serves a similar narrative function: Gilgamesh achieves everything a hero could desire (glory, friendship, adventure) and still confronts death and inadequacy. Qohelet's royal experiment and Gilgamesh's heroic one both end in the same diagnosis, though Qohelet has a theological resolution that Gilgamesh lacks.
11: Then I considered all that my hands had done — the post-experiment evaluation that is the classic wisdom reflection step. The cycle of testing, observing, and reflecting is the methodological structure of Ecclesiastes. The evaluation produces the hevel verdict: all the greatest human achievements, viewed from the perspective of death and transience, are vapor. The Mesopotamian Pessimistic Dialogue catalogs the same disappointment; Qohelet's difference is the theological frame of God's gifts that follows in vv24-26.
12: The succession problem — what can the man who comes after the king do? — reflects a real anxiety in ANE succession politics. Every new king inherited and evaluated his predecessor's achievements; Rehoboam's handling of Solomon's legacy (1 Kings 12) is the Israelite dramatization of what Qohelet describes. The frustration that the successor may undo the predecessor's work was a genuine royal concern across the ancient world, from Egypt to Mesopotamia.
13: Wisdom excels folly as light excels darkness — the unambiguous endorsement of wisdom despite the futility diagnosis. Qohelet is not a nihilist; he affirms the real advantage of wisdom while exposing its limits. The light/darkness contrast was a fundamental ANE wisdom polarity: Egyptian texts contrast the illuminated sage with the blind fool; Proverbs 4:18-19 uses the same contrast. Qohelet affirms the polarity without allowing it to answer the death-question.
14: The wise person has eyes in his head; the fool walks in darkness — yet I perceived that one fate comes to all. The word miqreh (fate, what befalls one) is what happens to both wise and fool: death. The observation that death is the universal human event was common in ancient wisdom: Job 21:23-26 notes that both the righteous and the wicked die alike. Qohelet's contribution is to place this observation within the comprehensive evaluation of wisdom's value.
15: What happens to the fool will happen to me also — the rhetorical move that exposes the wisdom tradition's limit. Psalm 73 addresses the same problem: until the psalmist enters the sanctuary and discerns the end of the wicked, he nearly loses his footing. Qohelet's honest wrestling with wisdom's limits is the wisdom tradition's self-critique, creating the space for the larger narrative of divine intervention — the resurrection that finally answers what wisdom alone cannot.
16: There is no lasting remembrance of the wise any more than of the fool — the erosion of memory as the ultimate leveler. Egyptian wisdom education aimed at producing a sage whose wisdom would be remembered: the Teaching of Ptahhotep hoped the student would be remembered for generations. Qohelet exposes the limit of this aspiration: even the famous teacher is forgotten. Only YHWH's memory is permanent; human forgetfulness erases even the most celebrated wisdom.
17: So I hated life — the existential nadir of the wisdom experiment. This is not suicidal despair but the honest evaluation that rigorous 'under the sun' analysis produces. The Mesopotamian Dialogue of a Man with His Soul reaches a similar desperate conclusion. Qohelet's willingness to follow the argument to its logical end — even to hating life — is what makes his subsequent turn toward God-given enjoyment (v24-26) theologically serious rather than superficial optimism.
18: I hated all my toil because I must leave it to the man who comes after me — the succession anxiety intensified. The ancient Near Eastern concern with inheritance and succession was profound: all major legal codes (Hammurabi, the Hittite laws) include extensive provisions for inheritance. Qohelet personalizes the problem: the unknown quality of the heir undermines the meaning of the predecessor's entire life project. Hezekiah's wealth passing to Manasseh is the historical illustration.
19: Who knows whether he will be wise or foolish? — the uncertainty of the heir's character exposes the fragility of all achievement. The succession narratives of Israel's kings dramatize this: Hezekiah (faithful) was followed by Manasseh (wicked); Josiah (righteous) by sons who undid his reforms. Proverbs 17:2 notes that a servant who acts wisely will rule over a son who acts shamefully. The uncertainty of succession was a pervasive anxiety in ancient Near Eastern royal ideology.
20: The giving of his heart up to despair over toil is the bottom of the wisdom experiment — full acknowledgment that the project has failed on its own terms. Ancient wisdom literature recognized this nadir: the Righteous Sufferer (Ludlul bel nemeqi) reaches a similar point before the divine intervention that resolves his situation. Qohelet reaches despair as a methodological conclusion; what follows (v24-26) is the theology of gift that transcends the experiment's failure.
21: One person labors with wisdom, knowledge, and skill, yet must hand everything to someone who has not toiled for it — the injustice of the succession problem. Ecclesiastes critiques the assumption that work-and-reward is the only framework for meaning. The NT corollary is that grace — receiving what one did not work for — is the basis of salvation, the gracious inversion of the merit/achievement framework that Qohelet exposes as inadequate.
22: What does a man get from all his toil? — the rhetorical return to the opening yitron question (Ecclesiastes 1:3). The repetition marks the structure: the experiment is complete; the question is re-posed before the theological answer is given. The ANE context: the Gilgamesh epic returns to the walls of Uruk at the end — Gilgamesh's tangible achievement is all that remains. Qohelet refuses this consolation: the walls are also hevel.
23: All his days are full of sorrow, and his work is grief; even at night his heart does not rest — the sleeplessness of anxious toil. Egyptian wisdom texts mention the restless man who cannot sleep as a picture of disordered priorities. Psalms 127:2 addresses the same point: it is in vain that you rise up early and go late to rest, eating the bread of anxious toil; for he gives sleep to his beloved. The remedy is not less work but differently oriented work — as gift from God, not as compulsive self-justification.
24: There is nothing better for a person than to eat and drink and find enjoyment in his toil — the first of several carpe diem passages (also Ecclesiastes 3:12-13, Ecclesiastes 3:22, Ecclesiastes 5:18-20, Ecclesiastes 8:15, Ecclesiastes 9:7-9). The closest ANE parallel is Siduri's speech to Gilgamesh: when the gods created mankind, they allotted death to mankind but life they retained in their own keeping; let your belly be full, make merry day and night. Qohelet shares the surface form but inverts the theology: enjoyment is not the consolation prize for mortality but is from the hand of God.
25: For apart from God, who can eat or find enjoyment? — the theological grounding that distinguishes Qohelet from the Gilgamesh parallel. The Mesopotamian carpe diem is resigned acceptance of fate; Qohelet's is the reception of divine gift. This makes all the difference: the enjoyment God gives is not escapism but a form of gratitude. Ben Sira (Sirach Ecclesiastes 14:16) picks up the same theme in Second Temple Judaism: treat yourself well before you die, for there is no pleasure in Sheol.
26: God gives wisdom, knowledge, and joy to the one who pleases him, but to the sinner he gives the business of gathering and accumulating, only to give to one who pleases God — the providential redistribution underlying the succession problem. This is the theological resolution of Ecclesiastes 2:18-23: the sinner's frantic accumulation ultimately enriches the person who pleases God. The background is covenant theology: YHWH's blessing rests on the faithful; what appears to be the wicked's gain is being gathered for the righteous. The meek shall inherit the earth (Matthew 5:5) is the eschatological form of this principle.
